title: Justice K.S. Puttaswamy v. Union of India (Right to Privacy Case)
legal_principles: Right to privacy is a fundamental right
summary: 9-judge bench unanimously declared that the right to privacy is a fundamental right under Article 21. Overruled M.P. Sharma (1954) and Kharak Singh (1962) which had held privacy is not a fundamental right. Paved the way for Aadhaar challenge and India's Personal Data Protection framework.
key_holdings: Right to privacy is a fundamental right protected under Article 21
Privacy includes autonomy over personal choices, informational privacy, and bodily integrity
State can restrict privacy only through law, legitimate aim, and proportionality
Data protection is a facet of right to privacy
Private entities can also violate privacy rights
key_quotes: Privacy is the ultimate expression of the sanctity of the individual.
The right to be let alone is the beginning of all freedom.
why_important: Landmark unanimous ruling declaring privacy a fundamental right. Foundation for India's data protection law and restraints on Aadhaar-based surveillance.
relevance_to_legal_finance_system: foundational for financial data privacy — bank account linking, PAN-Aadhaar, and RBI data localisation requirements.
